coredata.dc:title: Development of green photocatalyst using empty fruit bunches from Elaeis guineensis for methylene blue degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Rani, Umairah Abd
Ng, Law Yong
Ng, Ching Yin
Chiew, Chia Min
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
Ong, Chin Boon
Hairom, Nur Hanis Hayati
coredata.dc:description: The application of transition metals for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is expensive and impractical due to health and safety concerns. Thus, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (NCQDs) is studied in this work given their low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and renewable feedstock. However, the performance study using NCQDs produced from empty fruit bunches (EFB) through a hydrothermal treatment is not detailed in previous works. This study aims to evaluate the effects of temperature, pH, NCQDs dosage, and UV-light irradiation time on the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue using the Taguchi optimization approach with 3-factor levels. Based on the study, an optimum operating condition at 62.5 °C of solution temperature, solution pH of 10.7, 13.4 ml of NCQDs dosage, and 322.7 min of irradiation duration has successfully achieved 95.75% of methylene blue degradation efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Photocatalyst
Fluorescent
Degradation
Organic dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photodegradation study using the Taguchi method
Surface morphological analysis of CQDs and NCQDs
Elemental composition analysis of CQDs and NCQDs
Optical properties of CQDs and NCQDs
Photoluminescence properties of CQDs and NCQDs
Adsorption of methylene blue onto the NCQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman [ IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2020-C1/N03 ]; and UCSI University [REIG-FETBE-2020/066].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface morphologies of (a) CQDs and (b) NCQDs observed at 50X magnification.
Crystallinity structure of (a) CQDs and (b) NCQDs.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) CQDs and (b) NCQDs.
Photoluminescence profile of (a) CQDs and (b) NCQDs and the fluorescence appearance of (c) CQDs and (d) NCQDs under daylight and UV-light irradiations, respectively.
The MB dye removal efficiency through the adsorption process by NCQDs.
Mean of S/N ratios for three parameters in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye.
The photocatalytic degradation profile of MB dye versus irradiation time.
The degradation pathway of MB dye molecules by the fabricated NCQDs.
Experimental design for photodegradation study using the Taguchi approach.
Composition analysis of CQDs and NCQDs using EDX.
Photocatalytic degradation factors by NCQDs at different factorial levels.
Absorbance and removal efficiency of each experimental run.
Preliminary ANOVA results for the photocatalysis using NCQDs.
ANOVA results without considering the effect of MB solution temperature.
Significance of each factor for dye removal efficiency.

head.ce:title: Development of green photocatalyst using empty fruit bunches from Elaeis guineensis for methylene blue degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eFaculty of Engineering Technology, Universiti Tun Hussein Onn Malaysia, Hab Pendidikan Tinggi Pagoh, KM 1, Jalan Panchor, 84600, Muar, Johor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of transition metals for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is expensive and impractical due to health and safety concerns. Thus, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (NCQDs) is studied in this work given their low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and renewable feedstock. However, the performance study using NCQDs produced from empty fruit bunches (EFB) through a hydrothermal treatment is not detailed in previous works. This study aims to evaluate the effects of temperature, pH, NCQDs dosage, and UV-light irradiation time on the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue using the Taguchi optimization approach with 3-factor levels. Based on the study, an optimum operating condition at 62.5 °C of solution temperature, solution pH of 10.7, 13.4 ml of NCQDs dosage, and 322.7 min of irradiation duration has successfully achieved 95.75% of methylene blue degradation efficiency.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye contamination is particularly prominent in water pollution due to economic activities in textile industries, leather, cosmetics, and papers [1]. Methylene blue (MB) dye is a heterocyclic dye commonly found in industrial wastewater, which can cause environmental problems due to its high toxicity and non-biodegradable properties. MB dye wastes may also cause several complications to aquatic life and human health due to their long persistence in the environment and weak bio-decomposition properties. Various methods have been suggested for treating dye wastewater, including biological and chemical precipitation [2,3]. However, these methods have several restrictions, such as low biodegradability of the dye molecules, long duration required for effective removal, excessive chemical usage, and generation of a large amount of sludge [4,5].

Photocatalytic degradation is one of the effective techniques in the dye removal process due to its high performance in treating organic dyes, shorter operation duration, and inexpensive operational cost and equipment setup [6]. Semiconductors, such as titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, and cerium oxide, are the common photocatalysts used in dye removal [7–9]. Nevertheless, metallic photocatalyst production costs are relatively higher and may cause additional contamination to the water source if the photocatalysts are not adequately handled.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have distinctive properties such as outstanding photo-stability, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and strong photo-luminescence behavior [10,11]. NCQDs are one of the potential candidates for replacing the common metallic photocatalysts in the degradation of organic dyes. NCQDs have good optical performance in degrading organic dyes as they can act as electron donors or acceptors. However, the characteristics and performance optimization of NCQDs fabricated from empty fruit bunches in the photocatalytic degradation of MB have not been previously demonstrated. Thus, this study is aimed to characterize the NCQDs fabricated from the empty fruit bunches while optimizing the photodegradation condition using the methylene blue solution.

Empty fruit bunches (EFB) were collected from Seri Ulu Langat Palm Oil Mill Sdn Bhd, Malaysia. Methylene blue (C.l.52015) and urea (99%) were supplied by Merck Company. Sodium hydroxide pellets (97%) and hydrochloric acid (37%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: EFB fibers were washed for several cycles using distilled water to remove the undesired impurities. Then, 30 g of EFB fibers were added into 300 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.5 M) and heated up at 150 °C for 2 h. This process was aimed at breaking down the cell walls of EFB fibers and release the lignins from the internal content [12]. After that, the large EFB fibers were filtered out from the suspension using a filter paper with a pore size of 20–25 µm. Hydrochloric acid (at a concentration of 0.5 M) was added dropwise into the filtrate and stopped when the pH of the solution achieved pH 3. The mixture was left at room temperature for 24 h to allow the lignins to form precipitates. Afterward, the lignins were collected by vacuum filtration and placed into an oven at 70 °C for drying. Then, 2 g of the solid lignin and 0.5 g of urea were mixed with 100 ml of deionized water and stirred for 5 min. The mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated up at 180 °C for 8 h. The NCQDs in brownish liquid form was obtained at the end of the process. This study also produced CQDs without doping elements using the same fabrication method to study their comparative characterizations. The functionality of NCQDs in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye solution was evaluated.

Methylene blue (MB) solution of 1000 ppm was prepared as a stock solution. 29 sets of MB solutions at a concentration of 10 ppm were prepared. The entire process was conducted under dark conditions to avoid light degradation.

In order to investigate the adsorption capability, NCQDs solution was first diluted with a dilution factor of 1:10. Then, 10 ml of diluted NCQDs solution was added into the 100 mL of MB solution of 10 ppm that was previously prepared. The experimental work was conducted in the dark condition for an hour. Treated samples were collected for analyses using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM), Hitachi S-3400 N, was employed to study the surface morphologies. The images were generated by analyzing the signals from the reflected electron beams that strike the particle surfaces. The zeta potential analysis, however, was measured by dynamic light scattering (Malvern). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, Hitachi S-3400 N, was used in analyzing the elemental composition. EDX was used to estimate the atomic percentage of the sample. X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) (model Shimadzu XRD-6000) was used in analyzing the crystallinity in which the scanning range was between 0° and 80° Light absorption analysis was carried out by a double-beamed UV–Vis spectrophotometer (model Shimadzu UV-2600) at a resolution of 0.1 nm using a quartz cuvette with 10 mm of path length. The absorption wavelength was observed over a wide range of 200 to 800 nm. In order to determine the emission wavelength, photoluminescence spectroscopy (model Edinburgh Instrument FLS920) was used. The detection range was observed between 200 nm to 1000 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation study using the Taguchi method
ce:section.ce:para:
The Taguchi approach was employed in this study to determine the optimum condition for photodegradation of methylene blue dye. L9 (34) orthogonal array [13] was used to evaluate 4 independent factors, including solution temperature, solution pH, NCQDs dosage, and irradiation time at 3-factor levels. The initial dye concentration was constant at 10 ppm for each experimental run [14]. A total of 9 experimental sets (refer to Table 1 ) were conducted under 302 nm of UV lamp using Nyxtechnik UV box T12621D [15]. Each experimental run was repeated three times to obtain the averaged values. The mixtures of NCQDs and the degraded MB dye solutions were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 660 nm. Hence, the photodegradation efficiency of the dye can be calculated using Eq. (1).
(1) Dye removal efficiency (%)=(Co-Ct)/Cox100%
where Co is the initial concentration of dye solution and Ct is the concentration of dye solution at time t after the photodegradation process was started.
The confirmation runs were carried out to verify the accuracy and reliability of the optimum operating condition that was postulated based on the ANOVA results

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphological analysis of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Surface morphologies of the dried CQDs and NCQDs were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) presented in Fig. 1 (a-b), in which the particles had quite similar sizes with some aggregations [16]. The particle shapes were relatively uniform with spherical morphology. The images showed the effectiveness of the fabrication and modification processes in controlling the structures of the NCQDs using the EFB as the raw materials. The even heating process at constant time and temperature using a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave resulted in the formation of NCQDs with homogeneous morphology.

The morphology images with uniform particle sizes indicated that the NCQDs were successfully fabricated through the hydrothermal treatment, which is a promising technique for preparing nanostructured quantum dots with controllable size and morphology [17]. Both CQDs and NCQDs fabricated from EFB fibres showed relatively similar morphology. However, the surface morphology of NCQDs was slightly more uniform in shape probably due to the presence of nitrogen atoms, which was functionalized with more active sites, favouring the photocatalytic degradation activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elemental composition analysis of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition of CQDs and NCQDs was analyzed using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Table 2 . The result showed that the NCQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen atoms since the molecular structure of NCQDs consists of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine functional groups on their surfaces [18]. Meanwhile, the CQD structure only consists of carbon and oxygen atoms. The formations of the functional groups on the CQDs and NCQDs structures are contributed by the raw materials used during the fabrication and modification processes.

Surface functionalization of lignin during the hydrothermal treatment has modified their chemical properties, which led to the production of NCQD possessing carboxyl, carbonyl and oxygenated-functional groups [19], imparting excellent water solubility. Besides, surface passivation on the lignin molecules can enhance the fluorescence properties of NCQDs by forming several functional groups on the edge of NCQD's carbon core. The functional groups on the NCQD surfaces can contribute to the activation of electrons when exposed to UV light, enhancing the photocatalyst in the photocatalytic degradation activity.

Based on the EDX result, NCQDs have a relatively lower atomic percentage of oxygen atoms since nitrogen atoms displaced some oxygen atoms by forming carbon-nitrogen single bonds. A small amount of sodium and chloride elements was mainly due to the leftover sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid during the lignin extraction from EFB fibers.

The crystallinity phase and unit cell dimension for CQDs and NCQDs were determined using an X-ray diffraction approach. The X-ray diffraction pattern of both CQDs and NCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 (a-b). The XRD characteristic peaks at around
2 θ =
20 ∘
−
30 ∘
, indicating the scattering from the carbon core at the inter-planar spacing and the crystalline nature of quantum dots [20]. By referring to the X-ray diffraction pattern of the pure CQDs, a broad peak was observed at
2 θ = 16 .
82 ∘
, which was linked to a crystal plane of (100) due to the presence of amorphous nature of carbon with the sp2 crystal phase of carbon.

In addition, there were two peaks at 21.36˚ and 26.32˚ with crystalline planes of (001) and (002), respectively, attributed to the graphitic crystal phase in confirming the formation of CQDs and NCQDs. The sharp peak at 45.76° was attributed to a crystalline plane of (111), indicating the presence of the carbonyl group within CQDs and NCQDs.

Based on the XRD pattern for NCQDs, the peak located at
2 θ = 31 .
84 ∘
with a crystalline plane of (210) verified the presence of nitrogen atoms in the crystallinity of NCQDs, which are in good agreement with the reported study [21]. This showed that the nitrogen atom had been successfully doped onto the carbon quantum dots derived from the empty fruit bunches.

The high crystallinity of CQDs with an abundance of active surface sites was conducive to photo-absorption and redox reactions, leading to efficient degradation of organic pollutants in the wastewater. Higher crystallinity of CQDs and NCQDs could lead to higher photocatalytic degradation activity [22], in which more electron-hole pairs can be generated under UV-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrophotometer analysis was carried out to study the optical absorption properties of CQDs and NCQDs, which can be related to the transition of the π system on the surfaces of quantum dot products. Figure 3 a shows the UV–Vis spectrum for CQDs in which two absorption peaks can be observed within 200 to 450 nm.

Both optical absorption peaks of CQDs and NCQDs at around 280 nm could be attributed to n-π* transition of CO, indicating a successful oxidation of carbon-based nanoparticles as well as the presence of oxygen-functionalized groups. Besides, the optical absorption peak at around 200 nm could be attributed to π -π* transition of CC double bonds and CC single bonds, originated from the aromatic π system and the core sp2 carbon bonds.

NCQDs possess a unique light-induced electron transfer property. When a UV-light source irradiates NCQDs, electrons can be emitted in the form of photons. This may contribute to the oxidation and reduction processes during photocatalytic degradation [23,24]. In addition, due to the good electron transfer property of NCQDs, they could effectively accelerate the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and improve photocatalytic activity.

The difference in the formation of the UV–Vis spectrum for CQDs and NCQDs is due to the differences in surface defects on their surfaces. The fluorescent NCQDs are better than CQDs in terms of optical properties due to the presence of doping elements [25]. The optical properties suggest that NCQDs can be a powerful energy-transfer component in photocatalyst design because of their electron reservoirs and electron-mediating nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs and NCQDs can be related to the confinement in quantum dots [26]. According to Fig. 4 (a-b), the emission wavelength of CQDs and NCQDs can be observed at 485 nm and 438 nm, respectively. The PL spectra indicated that the luminescence properties of CQDs and NCQDs could be attributed to the functional groups on their surfaces [27].

The appearance of CQDs and NCQDs solution under visible and UV-light irradiation has been evaluated in this study. In visible light, the CQDs (a) and NCQDs (b) solutions existed in brownish liquid. A light blue fluorescent with high intensity can be seen when exposed to UV light. When the surfaces of the CQD solution receive UV-light irradiation, a portion of the electrons is excited into higher energy states. When the electrons in CQDs fall back to their ground state, a photon is emitted [28,29] due to the nanoscale quantum dot particles, resulting in fluorescence and quantum confinement [30]. The excellent fluorescent properties of NCQDs could be attributable to the nitrogen doping on the surfaces of carbon quantum dots, as the nitrogen atoms are electron donors in nature.

The presence of nitrogen atoms on the surfaces of NCQDs could lead to the formation of a new energy state on NCQDs surfaces. Thus, more electron-hole pairs can then be generated, which can be used to improve the photocatalytic degradation activity [18]. NCQDs possess high sensitivity toward UV light that can enhance photocatalytic performance. NCQDs can convert the desired wavelength from sunlight for photocatalysis applications [31,32]. The enhancement in photocatalytic performance can be explained by the nanoscale characteristics of NCQDs that can encourage oxidation reaction during photocatalysis [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of methylene blue onto the NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Two phases are involved in the dye removal process using the NCQDs: adsorption and photodegradation. The adsorption of MB molecules onto the surfaces of NCQDs was conducted in a dark condition for one hour before exposure to UV-light irradiation. In this study, the adsorption analysis was conducted before the photodegradation phase to effectively evaluate the photodegradation efficiency of NCQDs towards the MB molecules.

Figure 5 showed that the dye removal efficiency through the adsorption of MB molecules by NCQDs was less than 10%. The presence of the π-π stacking interaction between MB molecules and the aromatic domains of NCQDs could lead to adsorption [3]. Besides, the functional groups on the NCQDs surfaces have contributed to the MB dye adsorption due to the interaction forces between NCQDs and MB dye molecules via hydrogen bonding and strong electrostatic interactions [34]. Therefore, the presence of NCQDs could lead to the adsorption of the MB molecules onto the surfaces of NCQDs. In this work, the adsorption process was limited by the available surfaces on the NCQDs, which could be affected by the dosage of NCQDs used during the adsorption study.

The adsorption capability of NCQDs towards the MB dye molecules was relatively low due to the unstable N+, carbonyl, and aniline groups in the MB dye structure, which may affect the photocatalytic degradation activity in the following steps [35].

Besides, the particle sizes of NCQDs were relatively small. At the same time, the fabricated NCQDs were highly soluble in an aqueous medium, causing the rate of adsorption to be limited by the presence of functional groups on the surfaces of NCQDs. Therefore, a photocatalytic degradation study was conducted using NCQDs in the following step to evaluate the overall MB removal efficiency. The presence of NCQDs solution in the MB dye solution can increase the production of free radicals during the photocatalytic degradation activity when energy is transferred to the NCQDs via UV-light irradiation at a wavelength of 302 nm.

In this study, the Taguchi orthogonal array (OA) of the factorial design was employed using Design Expert Software 9.0 to investigate the influential variables in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye and the mutual interaction between the selected variables [36]. Taguchi's approach effectively analyses the experimental data and determines each factor's influence. The Taguchi approach can provide a standard orthogonal array to evaluate the effects of selected factors. In order to optimize the parameters affecting the photodegradation of MB molecules by the NCQDs fabricated in this study, four different factors were chosen for evaluation in this work, including the temperature of the MB solution, pH of the MB solution, NCQDs dosage, and UV-light irradiation duration. Each factor consists of 3 different levels, as shown in Table 3 .

The absorbance of the initial MB dye solution was analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer, which was recorded as 1.606. Then, the final absorbance of the MB dye solution, after the photocatalytic degradation by NCQDs, was analyzed using the UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The dye removal efficiency was then calculated based on the absorbance values obtained before and after the photocatalytic degradation by NCQDs. Three replicates improved the accuracy of each experimental run, and the averaged values were used in the calculations. The results of dye removal efficiency after the photocatalytic degradation processes by NCQDs are tabulated in Table 4 .

ANOVA is helpful in providing details regarding the significance of each factor on the dye removal performance through the photocatalytic degradation by NCQDs fabricated in this work. Based on the ANOVA system, the p-value generated from ANOVA analysis represents the most significant factor in the degradation study in which the value should be less than 0.05 [37]. The F-value is a ratio of the mean square error to the residual error that can be used to determine the significance of a factor toward the responses. A higher F-value in the ANOVA analysis would indicate a higher impact of that particular factor on a specific response.

Table 5 shows the parameters in this study: MB solution temperature, MB solution pH, NCQDs dosage, and UV-light irradiation duration, respectively. ANOVA analysis showed that the dye solution pH was the most influential parameter on the MB dye degradation. The MB solution temperature showed the lowest F-value, among other factors, and the p-value was more than 0.05. Hence, the MB solution temperature factor can be considered insignificant and excluded from the model development. A new set of ANOVA results was obtained to improve the model's accuracy in predicting the performance of the NCQDs in the photodegradation of the MB molecules.

Each factor in the new ANOVA analysis led to significant results based on the F-values and p-values, as shown in Table 6 . Based on the ANOVA result, the solution pH has a high impact on the dye removal performance (83.7967%), followed by the irradiation time of photodegradation (15.1246%) and the NCQDs dosage (0.9503%).

The S/N ratio is one of the parameters that can be used to measure the variation in the design model. The S/N ratio is applied to identify the optimum level of each factor by calculating the mean values (represents the desirable effect) and the standard deviation (represents the undesirable effect) of the data set [37]. The actual desired value was represented by the signal term, while the undesired factors measured were known as noise. The S/N ratios can be classified into three categories: smaller-the-better characteristics, larger-the-better characteristics, and nominal-the-best characteristics [38]. In this study, the larger-the-better characteristic is the most suitable measurement and is defined in Eq. (2):
(2)
S / N
= − 10 log 10
(
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
1
y
i
2
)
where yi is the experimental outcome and n is the experimental run number. The mean S/N ratio can estimate the significance of each factor.

According to Table 7 , solution pH has the highest delta value, indicating that the solution pH had the most significant impact on dye removal performance. However, the least important factor would be NCQDs dosage, which has the lowest delta value. In this work, the solution temperature effect was excluded because it is an insignificant factor based on the S/N ratio and ANOVA results due to the stability luminescence property of NCQDs for the temperature range evaluated originating from the stable quantum confinement effect [39].
Figure 6 shows the mean SN ratios for each factor corresponding to their respective levels, showing the larger-the-better characteristic in degrading MB dye solution.

Based on the S/N ratios, MB dye degradation efficiency was significantly improved in the alkaline solution (pH range from 8 to 10), indicating that the alkaline environment may increase hydroxyl ions concentration, which could neutralize the proton ions generated by photocatalysis, leading to a higher amount of reactive radical species [40]. In addition, the carbon atoms of MB dye can become electrophilic, leading to the presence of more hydroxyl ions, thus, enhancing the MB dye removal efficiency [41].

However, further increase in the pH of the MB dye solution (over pH 10), will reduce the efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation activity. This is because, at very high pH solution, decolorization activity will occur due to the high adsorption of MB dye molecules as the solution surrounding covered with the high amount of hydroxyl ions produced by sodium hydroxide solution [42]. Since MB dye molecules had a cationic configuration, their adsorption onto the NCQDs was more favorable in the alkaline solution. Therefore, in a highly alkaline solution (more than pH 10), the removal of MB dye molecules was less likely to be caused by photocatalytic degradation activity.

On the other hand, the degradation efficiency of MB dye solution was slightly lower in the acidic solution, which could be explained by the competition between the hydrogen ions and the cationic MB dye molecules during the hydroxylation process. In the acidic solution, the hydroxyl radicals tended o undergo a reversed reaction due to the large amount of positively charged hydrogen ions that created the repulsive forces between the NCQD surfaces and inhibited the attachment of dye molecules onto their surfaces [43].

Based on the photo-degradation duration, the longer the irradiation time, the greater the MB degradation efficiency. Long irradiation time by the UV-light source can continuously provide the excitation energy required for the NCQDs to generate photons and electrons, producing more radicals that can assist in the photocatalytic degradation of the MB molecules.

Extended irradiation time, however, might not be necessary since the photodegradation process can be stopped when the MB dye molecules are entirely degraded. Besides, the effect plot based on the NCQDs dosage showed that the degradation efficiency of MB molecules increased initially with an increase in NCQDs dosage. This could be due to the higher number of photo-generated electron-hole pairs that increased the number of hydroxyl radicals responsible for the dye degradation. Since the NCQDs had small particle sizes, a high number of active surface sites could enhance the redox potential of the conduction band electrons and valence band holes during the degradation activity.

The fabricated NCQDs can act as electron traps to promote charge transfer, thus reducing the recombination rate of photo-generated charges [1]. When a small amount of NCQDs was added to the mixture, there were more empty spaces between the neighboring NCQDs and MB molecules. Thus, light could be easily transmitted through the mixture, which could enhance the photodegradation of the MB molecules by NCQDs due to the high amount of energy in inducing the generation of photons and electrons in the NCQDs. Meanwhile, excessive NCQDs would decrease the photodegradation rate due to the limited charge transfer capability.

An excessive amount of NCQDs also could inhibit the penetration of UV light into the mixture, subsequently lowering the photodegradation efficiency. Therefore, there was a declining trend after an optimal dosage of NCQDs was exceeded, which may be explained by the deactivation of activated molecules by collision with ground-state molecules. When the NCQD's optimal dosage was surpassed, the degradation rate decreased due to the light scattering and screening effects while reducing the photocatalytic activity of the NCQDs. Parts of the NCQD surfaces probably became unavailable for photon absorption, thus bringing little stimulation to the catalytic reaction.

By analyzing all the significant factors based on ANOVA and S/N ratios, a linearized model can describe the photocatalytic degradation of MB molecules by NCQDs. The regression coefficient of the model is 0.9936, and the degradation efficiency (DE) calculation can be expressed in Eq. (3):
(3) DE=−24.09876+6.32509B+0.236545C+0.102966D

With Eq. (3), about 100 sets of optimizing solutions were provided by ANOVA analysis. The highest predicted degradation efficiency (99.0984%) with a constraint of solution at pH 10.7, 13.4 ml of NCQDs dosage, and 322.7 min of UV-irradiation time.

The degradation equation was verified through the confirmation test carried out under the optimal condition suggested by the Taguchi approach. For every 20 min interval, the sample was collected and analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The experimental removal efficiency at 320 min was 95.75%, while the predicted value from ANOVA was 99.09%. There was a 3.34% error in the developed predicted model, indicating that the degradation model developed is relatively accurate for the DE prediction. The obtained standard deviation was about 0.3, indicating that the value was relatively consistent with the average value.

The photodegradation profile is presented in Fig. 7 , showing the degradation rate of MB dye was inversely proportional to the irradiation time. This can be explained by the reduced amount of MB molecules when the photodegradation process was carried out at longer UV-light irradiation in the mixture's presence of the same amount of NCQDs. Therefore, the Taguchi method could determine the optimal parameters for addressing the desired performance of the photocatalytic degradation of MB molecules by NCQDs.

The photocatalytic mechanism can be categorized into two approaches: photo-sensation and narrow-sense photocatalytic degradation [44]. A transition of electrons initiates a photo-sensation process from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [45]. The excited electrons will then shift toward the conduction band and trigger the conversion of oxygen molecules (O2) into superoxide radicals (•O2 −). The entire process does not involve the formation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Thus, it is not suitable for explaining the catalytic mechanism of NCQDs in degrading the MB dye molecules in this work.

Two radicals, including •O2- and •OH, would be generated during the electron-hole pairs transmission for narrow-sense photocatalytic degradation. These radicals are essential in breaking the bondings of bulk molecules and transforming them into carbon dioxide and water molecules at the end of the degradation process. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of NCQDs in this study can be well described by the mechanism of narrow-sense photocatalytic degradation [46].

The degradation mechanism of MB dye molecules by NCQDs in this study is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Initially, NCQDs were irradiated by UV-light irradiation. The energy from the UV-light source was transmitted to the electrons that were present in the NCQDs. When the electrons have absorbed the energy from the light photons, the photoinduced electrons and holes are efficiently separated. They will be migrated from the ground state to the excited states of NCQD surfaces. The transmission of electron-hole pairs would transform the water and oxygen molecules into hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, which were helpful in degrading MB dye molecules [47,48]. Photogenerated holes would also attack MB molecules directly, leading to significant degradation of MB dye molecules.

Photocatalytic degradation of MB dye molecules is the direct absorption of a photon by the energy band gap of the NCQDs. In this study, the fabricated NCQDs could be an efficient photocatalyst due to the high density of electronic structure, and their increasing active surface area enlarged the range of wavelengths. As the NCQDs particle sizes were in the nanometer range, they could be easily activated by UV-light irradiation's absorption of light energy. In the absence of a UV-light source, photodegradation was not observed.

A complete cycle for the degradation of an MB molecule requires six types of transformation. In the primary degradation stage, the benzene ring and the amino group of the MB molecule would be attacked by hydroxyl radical, a process known as hydroxylation. Due to the presence of the hydroxyl groups, the electron density within the molecule became unstable while discharging some of the functional groups, such as methyl and amino groups. The tertiary and quaternary intermediates formed will be further attacked by the hydroxyl radicals formed.

As a result, the large bulk cyclic structure will be diminished into a smaller benzene group when the carbon-sulfur bond is no longer sustainable in preserving the stability of the existing structure. The superoxide and hydroxyl radicals will continuously break down the benzene ring until all the fragmented structures can be successfully reduced into less harmful compounds and ions, including carbon dioxide, water, sulfate, and nitrate [49]. The whole mechanism can be summarized in the following expression:
(4) NCQDs + hv → h+ + e −
(5) e− + O2 → •O2-
(6) h+ + H2O → H+ + •OH
(7) •OH + •O2− + MB → CO2 + H2O + SO4 2− + NO3 −

In this study, NCQDs have been successfully fabricated using empty fruit bunches as the raw material through a modified hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 8 h. NCQDs produced in this study are composed of granular particles with sizes of around a few nanometers. NCQDs developed from empty fruit bunches can be a sustainable candidate for the fabrication of future photocatalysts due to their small particle sizes that provide a large surface area in enhancing photodegradation efficiency.

The optimum conditions for the photodegradation of MB dye molecules by NCQDs fabricated by empty fruit bunches were successfully obtained by applying the Taguchi L9 (34) orthogonal array. The percentage of contribution from ANOVA analysis revealed that the solution pH was the most significant contributor to the model (83.76%). The optimum condition for the photodegradation of the MB molecules by the NCQDs was predicted to be at a solution of pH 10.7, 13.4 ml of NCQDs dosage, and 322.7 min of UV-irradiation duration to achieve 99.09% of MB removal efficiency. However, based on the results obtained through the confirmation runs, an average value of 95.75% of MB removal efficiency using the NCQDs was relatively consistent with a 3.34% percentage error.

The MB dye degradation activity was initiated when a UV-light source irradiated the NCQDs. The energy from the photon could be used to promote the formation of free radicals in the MB solution, which would further degrade the MB dye molecules through the hydroxylation process. NCQDs fabricated from EFB in this study displayed an excellent performance in the photodegradation of MB molecules. However, a few challenges still need to be investigated in future works, including the potential recovery approaches for the NCQDs and the potential applications of NCQDs for other purposes, such as the disinfection of pathogens and organic solvent odor removal from drinking water sources.

Umairah Abd Rani: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Law Yong Ng: Methodology, Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ching Yin Ng: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chia Min Chiew: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Ebrahim Mahmoudi: Conceptualization. Chin Boon Ong: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Nur Hanis Hayati Hairom: Writing – review & editing.
